statement: Controllers handle HTTP concerns only — request parsing, response formatting, status codes. Business logic lives in services or domain modules.
because: Business logic in controllers is HTTP-coupled, can't be reused from background jobs or CLI commands, and is hard to unit-test (every test needs a mock request and response). Splitting the responsibilities means business rules are framework-agnostic and testable in isolation.
if_violated: The same logic gets re-implemented every time it's needed from a non-HTTP context. The two implementations drift. A bug fixed in the controller version is not fixed in the cron job version. Customer reports a discrepancy, takes a week to root-cause.
instead: Controller calls a service function with parsed inputs. Service returns a typed result (data or domain error). Controller maps the result to an HTTP response. Service has zero awareness of HTTP.
